title: Build an E-Commerce Store
description: Create a full-featured online store with product catalog, shopping cart, multi-step checkout, Stripe payments, order management, search, SEO, analytics, and an admin dashboard using React with Redux and a Flask API.
keyPoints: Choose between monolith, microservices, and headless commerce architectures
Design a normalized product catalog with variants, categories, and inventory
Build a persistent shopping cart with abandoned cart recovery
Implement multi-step checkout with address validation and shipping options
Integrate Stripe Payment Intents, webhooks, and refund handling
Manage the full order lifecycle from pending to delivered
Add full-text search with faceted filters and autocomplete
Optimize performance with lazy loading, CDN, and image pipelines
Secure the store against CSRF, injection, and fraud
Boost discoverability with structured data, sitemaps, and Open Graph
Track conversions, funnels, and revenue with analytics events
Build an admin dashboard for products, orders, and inventory alerts

blocks.heading: E-Commerce Architecture
blocks.body: Before writing any code, you need to decide how your store is structured. The architecture you pick affects everything from deployment complexity to how fast you can ship new features. Over the past decade, e-commerce has moved from monolithic platforms to composable, headless systems, but each approach has legitimate trade-offs.

blocks.heading: Architecture Comparison
rows: Single codebase serves HTML, handles payments, manages inventory
Hard to scale individual pieces independently
Separate services for catalog, cart, orders, payments, each with own DB
Large teams, high traffic, complex domains
Operational overhead: service mesh, distributed tracing, eventual consistency
Backend API (or SaaS like Shopify/Saleor) with decoupled frontend
Need to build or integrate the entire frontend yourself
Static site generator pulls product data at build time, dynamic cart via API
Rebuild required for product updates; dynamic features need serverless functions

cards.title: Product Catalog
cards.body: Filterable grid with categories, variants, search, and inventory tracking.

cards.title: Shopping Cart
cards.body: Persistent cart with quantity controls, stock validation, and abandoned cart recovery.

cards.title: Checkout Flow
cards.body: Multi-step form with address validation, shipping options, and order summary.

cards.title: Stripe Payments
cards.body: Payment Intents API, webhook confirmation, refunds, and PCI compliance.

cards.title: Order Management
cards.body: Full lifecycle tracking, email notifications, and status history.

cards.title: Search & Discovery
cards.body: Full-text search, faceted filters, autocomplete, and relevance ranking.

cards.title: User Accounts
cards.body: Registration, login, order history, wishlists, and saved addresses.

cards.title: SEO & Analytics
cards.body: Structured data, dynamic sitemaps, conversion funnels, and A/B testing.

cards.title: Admin Dashboard
cards.body: Product CRUD, order management, inventory alerts, and sales reports.

blocks.title: This Guide Uses a Monolith
blocks.body: We build a monolithic Flask API with a React frontend. This is the fastest path to a working store. Every pattern shown here (cart state, payment flow, order lifecycle) transfers directly if you later split into microservices or adopt a headless CMS.

blocks.heading: Product Catalog: Database Schema
blocks.body: A well-designed product schema is the foundation of every e-commerce store. Products have variants (size, color), belong to hierarchical categories, carry multiple images, and need full-text search indexes. The schema below handles all of this with proper normalization and indexing.

blocks.description: Complete e-commerce database schema with categories, products, variants, tags, orders, reviews, wishlists, and coupons.
blocks.code:
-- Hierarchical categories with self-referencing parent
CREATE TABLE categories (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(60) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  slug VARCHAR(80) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  parent_id INTEGER REFERENCES categories(id),
  image_url TEXT,
  sort_order INTEGER DEFAULT 0
);
CREATE INDEX idx_categories_parent ON categories(parent_id);

-- Core product table
CREATE TABLE products (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  slug VARCHAR(220) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  description TEXT DEFAULT '',
  short_description VARCHAR(500) DEFAULT '',
  price DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL CHECK (price >= 0),
  compare_at_price DECIMAL(10,2),
  cost_price DECIMAL(10,2),
  category_id INTEGER REFERENCES categories(id),
  brand VARCHAR(100),
  images JSONB DEFAULT '[]',
  featured BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
  weight_grams INTEGER,
  metadata JSONB DEFAULT '{}',
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_category ON products(category_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_price ON products(price);
CREATE INDEX idx_products_active ON products(active) WHERE active = TRUE;
CREATE INDEX idx_products_featured ON products(featured) WHERE featured = TRUE;

-- Full-text search index on name and description
CREATE INDEX idx_products_search ON products
  USING GIN (to_tsvector('english', name || ' ' || description));

-- Product variants (size, color, etc.)
CREATE TABLE product_variants (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  product_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  sku VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  options JSONB DEFAULT '{}',
  price_override DECIMAL(10,2),
  stock INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (stock >= 0),
  low_stock_threshold INTEGER DEFAULT 5,
  barcode VARCHAR(50),
  weight_grams INTEGER,
  active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);
CREATE INDEX idx_variants_product ON product_variants(product_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_variants_sku ON product_variants(sku);
CREATE INDEX idx_variants_stock ON product_variants(stock) WHERE stock <= 5;

-- Tags for flexible product grouping
CREATE TABLE tags (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(60) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  slug VARCHAR(80) UNIQUE NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE product_tags (
  product_id INTEGER REFERENCES products(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  tag_id INTEGER REFERENCES tags(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  PRIMARY KEY (product_id, tag_id)
);

-- Orders
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id INTEGER REFERENCES users(id),
  email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
  status VARCHAR(30) DEFAULT 'pending',
  subtotal DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
  discount DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
  tax DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
  shipping_cost DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
  total DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
  shipping_address JSONB NOT NULL,
  billing_address JSONB,
  shipping_method VARCHAR(50),
  tracking_number VARCHAR(100),
  stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR(255),
  coupon_code VARCHAR(50),
  notes TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user ON orders(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_created ON orders(created_at DESC);

CREATE TABLE order_items (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  order_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  product_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id),
  variant_id INTEGER REFERENCES product_variants(id),
  product_name VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  variant_name VARCHAR(100),
  quantity INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0),
  unit_price DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL
);

-- Order status history for audit trail
CREATE TABLE order_status_history (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  order_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  old_status VARCHAR(30),
  new_status VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
  changed_by VARCHAR(100),
  note TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Reviews
CREATE TABLE reviews (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  product_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  user_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  rating INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5),
  title VARCHAR(120),
  body TEXT DEFAULT '',
  verified_purchase BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  UNIQUE (product_id, user_id)
);
CREATE INDEX idx_reviews_product ON reviews(product_id);

-- Wishlists
CREATE TABLE wishlists (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  product_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
  UNIQUE (user_id, product_id)
);

-- Coupons
CREATE TABLE coupons (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  code VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  discount_type VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL CHECK (discount_type IN ('percent', 'fixed')),
  discount_value DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
  min_order_amount DECIMAL(10,2) DEFAULT 0,
  max_uses INTEGER,
  used_count INTEGER DEFAULT 0,
  expires_at TIMESTAMP,
  active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Saved addresses
CREATE TABLE user_addresses (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
  label VARCHAR(50) DEFAULT 'Home',
  name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  address_line1 VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
  address_line2 VARCHAR(200),
  city VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  state VARCHAR(100),
  postal_code VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  country VARCHAR(2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'US',
  is_default BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

blocks.heading: Product Variants: Size, Color, and Beyond
blocks.body: Variants are the single hardest modeling problem in e-commerce. A t-shirt in 4 sizes and 3 colors is 12 SKUs, each with its own stock level and optional price override. The options JSONB column on product_variants stores the attribute key-value pairs (e.g., {"size": "L", "color": "Navy"}) so you can filter and display them without additional joins.

blocks.description: Product listing API with category filter, price range, full-text search, sorting, and pagination.
blocks.code:
from flask import Blueprint, request, jsonify
from db import get_db

products_bp = Blueprint("products", __name__)


@products_bp.route("", methods=["GET"])
def list_products():
    page = request.args.get("page", 1, type=int)
    per_page = min(request.args.get("per_page", 24, type=int), 100)
    category = request.args.get("category")
    min_price = request.args.get("min_price", type=float)
    max_price = request.args.get("max_price", type=float)
    sort = request.args.get("sort", "newest")
    search = request.args.get("q", "").strip()
    tag = request.args.get("tag")
    brand = request.args.get("brand")
    offset = (page - 1) * per_page

    where = ["p.active = TRUE"]
    params = []
    joins = []

    if category:
        where.append("c.slug = %s")
        params.append(category)
    if min_price is not None:
        where.append("p.price >= %s")
        params.append(min_price)
    if max_price is not None:
        where.append("p.price <= %s")
        params.append(max_price)
    if brand:
        where.append("p.brand ILIKE %s")
        params.append(brand)
    if tag:
        joins.append("JOIN product_tags pt ON pt.product_id = p.id")
        joins.append("JOIN tags t ON t.id = pt.tag_id")
        where.append("t.slug = %s")
        params.append(tag)
    if search:
        where.append(
            "to_tsvector('english', p.name || ' ' || p.description) "
            "@@ plainto_tsquery('english', %s)"
        )
        params.append(search)

    sort_map = {
        "newest": "p.created_at DESC",
        "price_asc": "p.price ASC",
        "price_desc": "p.price DESC",
        "name": "p.name ASC",
        "rating": "avg_rating DESC",
        "popular": "review_count DESC",
    }
    order_by = sort_map.get(sort, "p.created_at DESC")
    where_sql = " AND ".join(where)
    joins_sql = " ".join(joins)

    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        f"""
        SELECT p.id, p.name, p.slug, p.price, p.compare_at_price,
               p.images, p.brand, c.name AS category,
               COALESCE(AVG(r.rating), 0) AS avg_rating,
               COUNT(DISTINCT r.id) AS review_count
        FROM products p
        LEFT JOIN categories c ON c.id = p.category_id
        LEFT JOIN reviews r ON r.product_id = p.id
        {joins_sql}
        WHERE {where_sql}
        GROUP BY p.id, c.name
        ORDER BY {order_by}
        LIMIT %s OFFSET %s
        """,
        params + [per_page, offset],
    )
    cols = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
    products = [dict(zip(cols, row)) for row in cur.fetchall()]
    for p in products:
        p["avg_rating"] = float(p["avg_rating"])

    cur.execute(
        f"""
        SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT p.id)
        FROM products p
        LEFT JOIN categories c ON c.id = p.category_id
        {joins_sql}
        WHERE {where_sql}
        """,
        params,
    )
    total = cur.fetchone()[0]

    return jsonify(
        products=products,
        total=total,
        page=page,
        total_pages=(total + per_page - 1) // per_page,
    )


@products_bp.route("/<slug>", methods=["GET"])
def get_product(slug):
    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT p.*, c.name AS category_name,
               COALESCE(AVG(r.rating), 0) AS avg_rating,
               COUNT(DISTINCT r.id) AS review_count
        FROM products p
        LEFT JOIN categories c ON c.id = p.category_id
        LEFT JOIN reviews r ON r.product_id = p.id
        WHERE p.slug = %s
        GROUP BY p.id, c.name
        """,
        (slug,),
    )
    row = cur.fetchone()
    if not row:
        return jsonify(error="Product not found"), 404
    cols = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
    product = dict(zip(cols, row))
    product["avg_rating"] = float(product["avg_rating"])

    # Fetch variants
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT * FROM product_variants WHERE product_id = %s AND active = TRUE ORDER BY name",
        (product["id"],),
    )
    vcols = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
    product["variants"] = [dict(zip(vcols, v)) for v in cur.fetchall()]

    # Fetch reviews
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT r.id, r.rating, r.title, r.body, r.verified_purchase,
               r.created_at, u.display_name
        FROM reviews r JOIN users u ON u.id = r.user_id
        WHERE r.product_id = %s ORDER BY r.created_at DESC LIMIT 10
        """,
        (product["id"],),
    )
    rcols = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
    product["reviews"] = [dict(zip(rcols, rv)) for rv in cur.fetchall()]
    for rv in product["reviews"]:
        rv["created_at"] = rv["created_at"].isoformat()

    # Fetch tags
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT t.name, t.slug FROM tags t
        JOIN product_tags pt ON pt.tag_id = t.id
        WHERE pt.product_id = %s
        """,
        (product["id"],),
    )
    product["tags"] = [{"name": r[0], "slug": r[1]} for r in cur.fetchall()]

    return jsonify(product)

blocks.heading: Inventory Tracking
blocks.body: Stock lives on the variant, not the product. Every add-to-cart checks available stock, and every successful payment decrements it atomically inside a database transaction. The low_stock_threshold column drives admin alerts so you can reorder before you run out.

blocks.description: Inventory check and low-stock alert endpoints.
blocks.code:
from flask import Blueprint, jsonify
from flask_jwt_extended import jwt_required
from db import get_db

inventory_bp = Blueprint("inventory", __name__)


@inventory_bp.route("/check/<int:variant_id>", methods=["GET"])
def check_stock(variant_id):
    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT stock, low_stock_threshold FROM product_variants WHERE id = %s",
        (variant_id,),
    )
    row = cur.fetchone()
    if not row:
        return jsonify(error="Variant not found"), 404
    stock, threshold = row
    return jsonify(
        variant_id=variant_id,
        stock=stock,
        low_stock=stock <= threshold,
        available=stock > 0,
    )


@inventory_bp.route("/low-stock", methods=["GET"])
@jwt_required()
def low_stock_report():
    """Admin endpoint: list all variants at or below their threshold."""
    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT pv.id, pv.sku, pv.name AS variant_name, pv.stock,
               pv.low_stock_threshold, p.name AS product_name
        FROM product_variants pv
        JOIN products p ON p.id = pv.product_id
        WHERE pv.stock <= pv.low_stock_threshold AND pv.active = TRUE
        ORDER BY pv.stock ASC
        """
    )
    cols = [d[0] for d in cur.description]
    items = [dict(zip(cols, row)) for row in cur.fetchall()]
    return jsonify(low_stock_items=items, count=len(items))

blocks.heading: Shopping Cart
blocks.body: The cart is the most interactive piece of any store. You need to decide where cart state lives, how it persists across sessions, and how to handle edge cases like out-of-stock items and quantity limits. There are two main approaches.

blocks.heading: Cart Storage: Client vs Server
rows: Cart stored as JSON in the browser
Works without login, zero latency, simple
Lost if user clears storage, not synced across devices
Cart stored in DB or Redis, keyed by user ID or session token
Synced across devices, survives browser changes, enables abandoned cart emails
Requires auth or session management, adds network latency
localStorage for guests, merge into server cart on login
More complex merge logic when guest cart meets existing server cart

blocks.description: Cart hook using useReducer with localStorage persistence, stock validation, and computed totals.

blocks.heading: Abandoned Cart Recovery
blocks.body: Roughly 70% of online shopping carts are abandoned before checkout. Server-side cart storage lets you send recovery emails. The strategy is simple: a scheduled job finds carts older than one hour that never converted to an order, and sends a reminder email with a direct link back to checkout.

blocks.description: Scheduled task to find and email users with abandoned carts.
blocks.code:
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from db import get_db
from utils.email import send_email


def recover_abandoned_carts():
    """Run via cron every hour. Finds server-side carts
    older than 1 hour with no matching order and sends
    a single reminder email per cart."""
    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()
    cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=1)

    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT sc.id, sc.user_id, u.email, u.display_name,
               sc.items, sc.updated_at
        FROM server_carts sc
        JOIN users u ON u.id = sc.user_id
        WHERE sc.updated_at < %s
          AND sc.reminder_sent = FALSE
          AND sc.converted = FALSE
          AND jsonb_array_length(sc.items) > 0
        """,
        (cutoff,),
    )
    carts = cur.fetchall()

    for cart_id, user_id, email, name, items, updated in carts:
        item_count = len(items)
        send_email(
            to=email,
            subject="You left items in your cart!",
            template="abandoned_cart",
            context={
                "name": name,
                "item_count": item_count,
                "cart_url": f"https://store.example.com/cart?recover={cart_id}",
            },
        )
        cur.execute(
            "UPDATE server_carts SET reminder_sent = TRUE WHERE id = %s",
            (cart_id,),
        )

    db.commit()
    return len(carts)

blocks.heading: Checkout Flow
blocks.body: Checkout converts a cart into an order. The standard flow is: review cart, enter shipping address, select shipping method, enter payment, and confirm. Each step validates before advancing. Never create the Stripe Payment Intent until the user is ready to pay, because intents that are never confirmed still show up in your Stripe dashboard.

blocks.title: Review Cart
blocks.body: Show all cart items with quantities, prices, and subtotal. Validate stock one more time before proceeding. If any item is out of stock, show a warning and prevent advancing.

blocks.title: Shipping Address
blocks.body: Collect name, street address, city, state, ZIP, and country. If the user is logged in, offer their saved addresses as a dropdown. Validate ZIP format and required fields before advancing.
blocks.code:
const ADDRESS_FIELDS = [
  { name: 'name', label: 'Full Name', required: true },
  { name: 'address_line1', label: 'Street Address', required: true },
  { name: 'address_line2', label: 'Apt / Suite', required: false },
  { name: 'city', label: 'City', required: true },
  { name: 'state', label: 'State', required: true },
  { name: 'postal_code', label: 'ZIP Code', required: true, pattern: /^\d{5}(-\d{4})?$/ },
  { name: 'country', label: 'Country', required: true },
];

blocks.title: Shipping Method
blocks.body: After the address is entered, fetch available shipping rates. Display each option with name, estimated delivery date, and cost. Free shipping thresholds (e.g., free over $50) are applied server-side.

blocks.title: Payment
blocks.body: Create the order and Stripe Payment Intent on the server, then render Stripe Elements on the client for secure card input. The card number never touches your server, keeping you PCI compliant.

blocks.title: Order Confirmation
blocks.body: On successful payment, clear the cart, show the order number, and display estimated delivery. The Stripe webhook (not the client) is what actually marks the order as paid, so this page is optimistic UI. If the webhook has not arrived yet, show a 'Payment processing' message and poll for status.

blocks.description: Complete multi-step checkout with shipping form, shipping method selection, Stripe payment, and order confirmation.

blocks.heading: Payment Integration: Stripe
blocks.body: Stripe is the standard for online payments. The Payment Intents API is the modern approach: you create an intent on the server, pass the client secret to the browser, and Stripe.js handles the card form. Your server never sees raw card numbers, which keeps you PCI compliant with the simplest SAQ-A questionnaire.

blocks.heading: Stripe Integration Checklist
items: Install stripe on the backend (pip install stripe) and @stripe/react-stripe-js on the frontend (npm install @stripe/stripe-js @stripe/react-stripe-js)
Store STRIPE_SECRET_KEY and STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET in environment variables, never in code
Create a Payment Intent on the server when the user reaches the payment step, not before
Use Stripe Elements (CardElement) on the frontend so card data never touches your server
Confirm the payment on the client with stripe.confirmCardPayment(clientSecret)
Set up a webhook endpoint to receive payment_intent.succeeded and payment_intent.payment_failed events
Always verify the webhook signature to prevent spoofed events
Mark the order as paid only inside the webhook handler, never based on client-side confirmation alone
Add idempotency keys to Payment Intent creation to prevent duplicate charges on retries

blocks.description: Order creation with Stripe Payment Intent, stock validation, coupon support, and webhook handler with refund endpoint.
blocks.code:
import json
import os
from flask import Blueprint, request, jsonify
from flask_jwt_extended import jwt_required, get_jwt_identity
from db import get_db
from utils.email import send_order_confirmation
import stripe

stripe.api_key = os.getenv("STRIPE_SECRET_KEY")

orders_bp = Blueprint("orders", __name__)


@orders_bp.route("", methods=["POST"])
@jwt_required()
def create_order():
    user_id = int(get_jwt_identity())
    data = request.get_json()
    items = data["items"]
    shipping = data["shipping_address"]
    shipping_method = data.get("shipping_method", "standard")
    coupon_code = data.get("coupon_code")

    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()

    # --- Validate stock and calculate subtotal ---
    subtotal = 0
    validated = []
    for item in items:
        if item.get("variant_id"):
            cur.execute(
                """
                SELECT pv.stock, COALESCE(pv.price_override, p.price) AS price,
                       p.name, pv.name AS variant_name
                FROM product_variants pv
                JOIN products p ON p.id = pv.product_id
                WHERE pv.id = %s AND pv.product_id = %s
                """,
                (item["variant_id"], item["product_id"]),
            )
        else:
            cur.execute(
                "SELECT 9999, price, name, NULL FROM products WHERE id = %s",
                (item["product_id"],),
            )
        row = cur.fetchone()
        if not row:
            return jsonify(error=f"Product {item['product_id']} not found"), 400
        stock, price, name, variant_name = row
        if item["quantity"] > stock:
            return jsonify(error=f"{name} only has {stock} units in stock"), 400
        subtotal += float(price) * item["quantity"]
        validated.append({
            **item,
            "unit_price": float(price),
            "product_name": name,
            "variant_name": variant_name,
        })

    # --- Apply coupon ---
    discount = 0
    if coupon_code:
        cur.execute(
            """
            SELECT id, discount_type, discount_value, min_order_amount,
                   max_uses, used_count, expires_at, active
            FROM coupons WHERE code = %s
            """,
            (coupon_code,),
        )
        coupon = cur.fetchone()
        if coupon:
            c_id, c_type, c_val, c_min, c_max, c_used, c_exp, c_active = coupon
            if (
                c_active
                and (c_max is None or c_used < c_max)
                and subtotal >= c_min
            ):
                if c_type == "percent":
                    discount = round(subtotal * (float(c_val) / 100), 2)
                else:
                    discount = min(float(c_val), subtotal)
                cur.execute(
                    "UPDATE coupons SET used_count = used_count + 1 WHERE id = %s",
                    (c_id,),
                )

    # --- Shipping cost ---
    shipping_costs = {"standard": 0 if subtotal >= 50 else 5.99,
                      "express": 12.99, "overnight": 24.99}
    shipping_cost = shipping_costs.get(shipping_method, 5.99)

    tax = round((subtotal - discount) * 0.08, 2)
    total = round(subtotal - discount + tax + shipping_cost, 2)

    # --- Create Stripe Payment Intent ---
    intent = stripe.PaymentIntent.create(
        amount=int(total * 100),
        currency="usd",
        metadata={"user_id": str(user_id)},
        idempotency_key=f"order-{user_id}-{int(subtotal*100)}-{len(items)}",
    )

    # --- Insert order ---
    cur.execute(
        """
        INSERT INTO orders
            (user_id, email, subtotal, discount, tax, shipping_cost,
             total, shipping_address, shipping_method,
             stripe_payment_intent_id, coupon_code)
        VALUES (%s, (SELECT email FROM users WHERE id = %s),
                %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s)
        RETURNING id
        """,
        (user_id, user_id, subtotal, discount, tax, shipping_cost,
         total, json.dumps(shipping), shipping_method,
         intent.id, coupon_code),
    )
    order_id = cur.fetchone()[0]

    for item in validated:
        cur.execute(
            """
            INSERT INTO order_items
                (order_id, product_id, variant_id, product_name,
                 variant_name, quantity, unit_price)
            VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s, %s)
            """,
            (order_id, item["product_id"], item.get("variant_id"),
             item["product_name"], item.get("variant_name"),
             item["quantity"], item["unit_price"]),
        )

    # Log initial status
    cur.execute(
        """
        INSERT INTO order_status_history (order_id, new_status, changed_by)
        VALUES (%s, 'pending', 'system')
        """,
        (order_id,),
    )

    db.commit()
    return jsonify(
        order_id=order_id,
        client_secret=intent.client_secret,
        total=total,
    ), 201


@orders_bp.route("/webhook", methods=["POST"])
def stripe_webhook():
    payload = request.get_data()
    sig = request.headers.get("Stripe-Signature")
    webhook_secret = os.getenv("STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET")

    try:
        event = stripe.Webhook.construct_event(payload, sig, webhook_secret)
    except (ValueError, stripe.error.SignatureVerificationError):
        return jsonify(error="Invalid signature"), 400

    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()

    if event["type"] == "payment_intent.succeeded":
        pi_id = event["data"]["object"]["id"]

        # Mark order as paid
        cur.execute(
            "UPDATE orders SET status='paid', updated_at=NOW() "
            "WHERE stripe_payment_intent_id=%s AND status='pending'",
            (pi_id,),
        )

        # Decrement inventory atomically
        cur.execute(
            """
            UPDATE product_variants pv
            SET stock = pv.stock - oi.quantity
            FROM order_items oi
            JOIN orders o ON o.id = oi.order_id
            WHERE o.stripe_payment_intent_id = %s
              AND oi.variant_id = pv.id
            """,
            (pi_id,),
        )

        # Log status change
        cur.execute(
            """
            INSERT INTO order_status_history (order_id, old_status, new_status, changed_by)
            SELECT id, 'pending', 'paid', 'stripe_webhook'
            FROM orders WHERE stripe_payment_intent_id = %s
            """,
            (pi_id,),
        )

        # Send confirmation email
        cur.execute(
            """
            SELECT o.id, o.total, o.email,
                   json_agg(json_build_object(
                       'name', oi.product_name,
                       'quantity', oi.quantity,
                       'price', oi.unit_price
                   )) AS items
            FROM orders o
            JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
            WHERE o.stripe_payment_intent_id = %s
            GROUP BY o.id
            """,
            (pi_id,),
        )
        row = cur.fetchone()
        if row:
            send_order_confirmation(row[2], row[0], float(row[1]), row[3])

        db.commit()

    elif event["type"] == "payment_intent.payment_failed":
        pi_id = event["data"]["object"]["id"]
        cur.execute(
            "UPDATE orders SET status='payment_failed', updated_at=NOW() "
            "WHERE stripe_payment_intent_id=%s",
            (pi_id,),
        )
        db.commit()

    return jsonify(received=True), 200


@orders_bp.route("/<int:order_id>/refund", methods=["POST"])
@jwt_required()
def refund_order(order_id):
    """Admin endpoint to issue a full or partial refund."""
    data = request.get_json()
    amount_cents = data.get("amount_cents")  # None = full refund

    db = get_db()
    cur = db.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT stripe_payment_intent_id, total, status FROM orders WHERE id = %s",
        (order_id,),
    )
    row = cur.fetchone()
    if not row:
        return jsonify(error="Order not found"), 404
    pi_id, total, status = row

    if status not in ("paid", "processing", "shipped"):
        return jsonify(error=f"Cannot refund order in '{status}' status"), 400

    refund_params = {"payment_intent": pi_id}
    if amount_cents:
        refund_params["amount"] = amount_cents

    refund = stripe.Refund.create(**refund_params)

    new_status = "refunded" if not amount_cents else status
    cur.execute(
        "UPDATE orders SET status=%s, updated_at=NOW() WHERE id=%s",
        (new_status, order_id),
    )
    cur.execute(
        """
        INSERT INTO order_status_history
            (order_id, old_status, new_status, changed_by, note)
        VALUES (%s, %s, %s, 'admin', %s)
        """,
        (order_id, status, new_status,
         f"Refund {refund.id}: {refund.amount / 100:.2f} USD"),
    )
    db.commit()

    return jsonify(refund_id=refund.id, status=refund.status)

blocks.title: Stripe Test Mode
blocks.body: Always use Stripe test keys during development. The test card number 4242 4242 4242 4242 with any future expiry and any CVC simulates a successful payment. Use 4000 0000 0000 0002 to simulate a decline. Never commit real API keys to version control.

blocks.title: PCI Compliance
blocks.body: Never log, store, or transmit raw card numbers. By using Stripe Elements, card data goes directly from the browser to Stripe's servers, and your backend only sees tokens and Payment Intent IDs. This qualifies you for PCI SAQ-A, the simplest compliance level. If you ever handle raw card data yourself, you must complete a full PCI DSS audit.

blocks.heading: Order Management
blocks.body: An order moves through a lifecycle: pending, paid, processing, shipped, delivered. Each transition is logged in order_status_history so you have a full audit trail. Admin actions (packing, shipping, refunding) trigger status changes and can fire email notifications at each step.
